---
title: "Coordinate Descent: One Variable at a Time"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Optimization"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article,
# matched to the rest of the Optimization track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

CURVE   = "#2b3a67"    # the objective / surfaces
PATH    = "#c1121f"    # the iterate path / coordinate steps
ACCENT  = "#0a7d4d"    # highlights / the optimum
GD      = "#6c757d"    # gradient descent, for contrast
GREY    = "#6c757d"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is one of the two **algorithm articles** where the
> convexity spine and the descent spine reconverge. It assumes [OPTM-02 *Minimizing in
> Many Variables*] (the gradient, partial derivatives, the stationarity condition
> $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$), [OPTM-06 *Gradient Descent*] (the descent mindset, and the rival
> algorithm we will compare against throughout), and [OPTM-05 *Subgradients*] for one
> result we will lean on hard: the **subgradient optimality condition** $0\in\partial f$
> and the **soft-thresholding** solution of the scalar lasso subproblem. Because OPTM-05 is
> the owner of soft-thresholding, we restate and re-derive it inline here (@sec-soft) so
> this article reads end-to-end without a forward dependency you cannot follow.
>
> **The one question.** Gradient descent moves *every* variable at once, along the single
> steepest-descent direction. **What if we instead freeze all variables but one, minimize
> over that one alone, and cycle?** When is that smart, when is it the *only* easy option,
> and when does it stall?
>
> **What it sets up.** This article **supplies** the *coordinate descent* prerequisite that
> Regression's regularization articles ([REGR-15] lasso, [REGR-16] elastic net) link here
> for — coordinate descent *is* the standard lasso solver, and we derive exactly why. It
> also feeds [OPTM-16 *Proximal Methods*], which generalizes the per-coordinate
> soft-thresholding step into the full proximal-gradient picture, and it is the prototype
> for the **alternating minimization** that Unsupervised Learning links here for (k-means,
> matrix factorization).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Gradient descent ([OPTM-06]) commits to a single global direction, $-\nabla f$, and steps
all coordinates together. **Coordinate descent** makes the opposite bet: hold every
variable fixed except one, solve the resulting *one-dimensional* problem exactly (or take
one cheap step in it), advance to the next variable, and cycle. This article derives the
scheme from scratch, works out the exact per-coordinate update for a quadratic (a single
division — no step size to tune), and proves that for a **smooth, strongly convex**
objective the cyclic scheme drives the gradient to zero. Then it confronts the honest
catch: on a *non-smooth* objective, coordinate descent can **jam** at a non-minimizer
where no single-coordinate move helps but a diagonal move would — we build the classic
counterexample and see exactly why it happens. The payoff is the resolution of that
tension: when the non-smooth part is a **separable** penalty — a sum of one-dimensional
pieces, like the lasso's $\lambda\|\boldsymbol\beta\|_1$ — the jam cannot occur, each
coordinate update becomes **soft-thresholding** (derived in full), and coordinate descent
becomes the fast, warm-startable, sparsity-exploiting workhorse behind `glmnet` and every
production lasso/elastic-net solver. We close with the honest comparison to gradient
descent: when the cheap exact 1-D step and the separable structure are present, coordinate
descent wins; when the variables are tightly coupled and there is no closed-form coordinate
solve, it is the wrong tool.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
To minimize $f(\mathbf{x})$ over $\mathbf{x}=(x_1,\dots,x_p)$, **change one coordinate at
a time.** Fix every coordinate but the $j$-th at its current value and solve the
one-dimensional problem
$$
x_j \;\leftarrow\; \arg\min_{t}\; f(x_1,\dots,x_{j-1},\,t,\,x_{j+1},\dots,x_p),
$$
then move to the next coordinate and repeat, cycling $j=1,2,\dots,p,1,2,\dots$ until
nothing changes. There is **no global step size to tune**: each 1-D subproblem is solved
on its own terms, often in closed form by a single division (a quadratic) or a single
clipping operation (the lasso). The scheme works when two things hold: (1) each 1-D
subproblem is *easy*, and (2) the non-smooth part of $f$, if any, is **separable** across
coordinates — a sum $\sum_j g_j(x_j)$. Smoothness alone, or separability alone, is enough
to make it converge; lacking both, it can **stall at a non-minimizer**. The lasso sits
exactly in the sweet spot, which is why coordinate descent is its standard solver.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The scheme is almost embarrassingly simple — "optimize
$x_1$, then $x_2$, then $x_3$, …" — and the entire intellectual content is in the two
conditions: *when does cycling through cheap 1-D solves actually reach the joint minimum,
and when does it get trapped?* The answer turns on one geometric fact about non-smooth
functions, and the lasso is the headline case where that fact breaks the right way.

# Why we are here: the curse of moving everything at once {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view the whole way through: **when is it better to make many small,
exact, cheap moves along the axes than one expensive move along the gradient?** Every step
below answers a piece of that.

## What gradient descent costs, and what it cannot do {#sec-gd-costs}

[OPTM-06] gave us the universal fallback when $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ has no closed form: the
step $\mathbf{x}_{k+1}=\mathbf{x}_k-\eta\,\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$. It is general and it
works, but it carries three burdens that motivate an alternative.

1. **It needs the full gradient.** Every step computes all $p$ partial derivatives, even
   when $p$ is in the tens of thousands (genomics, text, recommender systems). For some
   objectives the full gradient is genuinely expensive to assemble.
2. **It needs a step size $\eta$.** The whole drama of [OPTM-06] and [OPTM-07] was choosing
   $\eta$: too small and it crawls, too large and it diverges, and the safe choice
   $\eta\le 1/L$ depends on a smoothness constant $L$ you usually do not know. A method that
   *eliminates* the step size would be a real prize.
3. **It chokes on a kink.** [OPTM-06] ended at the honest boundary: at a non-differentiable
   point — the corner of $|x|$, the vertex of the lasso penalty — the gradient does not
   exist, and plain gradient descent chatters or stalls. The lasso, the single most common
   sparse-regression tool, is *defined* by such a penalty.

Coordinate descent attacks all three at once. By working one variable at a time, it
touches one partial derivative per micro-step; by solving each 1-D subproblem *exactly*, it
sidesteps the step size entirely (the exact solve has no $\eta$); and — the payoff — when
the kink is *separable* across coordinates, each 1-D subproblem has its *own* kink that we
can solve in closed form, so the corner that defeats gradient descent becomes a feature.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"You already have gradient descent — why introduce a second first-order method?"* Because
the two make opposite assumptions and shine in opposite regimes. Gradient descent assumes
you can cheaply form the whole gradient and that the objective is smooth; it is direction-
optimal but coordinate-blind. Coordinate descent assumes each *single-variable* subproblem
is cheap and exactly solvable; it is coordinate-optimal but direction-blind. On a separable,
sparse, high-dimensional problem with a closed-form 1-D solve — the lasso — coordinate
descent is dramatically faster and handles the non-smoothness for free. On a dense, tightly
coupled problem with no cheap coordinate solve, gradient descent (or Newton, [OPTM-09]) is
the right call. Knowing *which* is the practitioner judgment this article is built to give.
:::

## The historical thread {#sec-history}

The idea is older than gradient descent's modern fame. **Gauss–Seidel** iteration
(mid-1800s) for solving a linear system $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ is *exactly* coordinate
descent applied to the quadratic $\tfrac12\mathbf{x}^\top A\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{b}^\top\mathbf{x}$:
update one component of $\mathbf{x}$ at a time using the most recent values of the others.
For a century it lived as a linear-algebra solver. Its second life came from statistics:
once the **lasso** (Tibshirani, 1996) made sparse regression central, the question of *how
to actually solve it* became urgent. The breakthrough — Friedman, Hastie, and Tibshirani's
`glmnet` (2007–2010) — was the recognition that **cyclic coordinate descent with a
soft-thresholding update**, run along a path of penalty values with warm starts, solves the
lasso and elastic net faster than any competitor of its day. That algorithm is the reason
coordinate descent is a first-class citizen of modern statistical computing, and deriving it
is the destination of this article (@sec-lasso).

# Intuition before symbols: walking the grid lines {#sec-intuition}

Picture the contour map of a function of two variables — concentric loops around the
minimum, exactly as in [OPTM-06]. Gradient descent steps **perpendicular to the contours**,
in whatever diagonal direction is steepest. Coordinate descent is forbidden diagonals: it
may only move **horizontally** (adjust $x_1$, hold $x_2$) or **vertically** (adjust $x_2$,
hold $x_1$). Each move goes as far as it usefully can along its allowed line — to the point
where that horizontal or vertical line is *tangent* to a contour, because that tangency is
exactly where the 1-D slice bottoms out.

The result is a **staircase**: a horizontal segment, then a vertical one, then horizontal
again, each leg ending tangent to a contour. @fig-staircase shows it. When the contours are
round (or axis-aligned ellipses), the staircase marches efficiently to the center. When the
contours are *tilted, narrow* ellipses — strong coupling between the variables — the
staircase takes tiny, cramped steps, the same pathology that makes gradient descent zig-zag,
and for the same reason: no axis-aligned move can make much progress along a diagonal valley.

```{python}
#| label: fig-staircase
#| fig-cap: "Coordinate descent as a staircase on the contour map. **Left:** a well-conditioned quadratic (nearly round contours). Each leg moves along one axis until it is tangent to a contour (the exact 1-D minimum along that line), and the staircase reaches the minimum quickly. **Right:** an ill-conditioned, *tilted* quadratic — a narrow diagonal valley. The same allowed moves now make painfully small progress, because no horizontal or vertical step can travel far down a diagonal trough. Coupling between coordinates is coordinate descent's enemy, exactly as it is gradient descent's."
def coord_descent_quadratic(A, b, x0, n_sweeps):
    """Exact cyclic coordinate descent on f = 1/2 x'Ax - b'x. Returns the path."""
    x = x0.astype(float).copy()
    path = [x.copy()]
    p = len(x)
    for _ in range(n_sweeps):
        for j in range(p):
            # exact 1-D min in x_j: A_jj x_j + sum_{k != j} A_jk x_k - b_j = 0
            x[j] = (b[j] - (A[j] @ x - A[j, j] * x[j])) / A[j, j]
            path.append(x.copy())
    return np.array(path)

def contour_and_path(ax, A, b, path, title):
    g = np.linspace(-3.2, 3.2, 200)
    X1, X2 = np.meshgrid(g, g)
    F = 0.5 * (A[0,0]*X1**2 + 2*A[0,1]*X1*X2 + A[1,1]*X2**2) - (b[0]*X1 + b[1]*X2)
    ax.contour(X1, X2, F, levels=18, colors=GREY, linewidths=0.6, alpha=0.6)
    ax.plot(path[:, 0], path[:, 1], "-o", color=PATH, ms=3.5, lw=1.6)
    xstar = np.linalg.solve(A, b)
    ax.scatter(*xstar, color=ACCENT, s=90, zorder=5, edgecolor="white", label="minimum")
    ax.scatter(*path[0], color=CURVE, s=55, zorder=5, label="start")
    ax.set_title(title); ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
    ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)

A_round = np.array([[3.0, 0.4], [0.4, 2.6]])
A_tilt  = np.array([[3.0, 2.85], [2.85, 3.0]])
b = np.array([1.0, -0.5])
x0 = np.array([-2.6, 2.6])

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 4.1))
contour_and_path(axL, A_round, b, coord_descent_quadratic(A_round, b, x0, 4),
                 "Well-conditioned: efficient staircase")
contour_and_path(axR, A_tilt, b, coord_descent_quadratic(A_tilt, b, x0, 12),
                 "Ill-conditioned: cramped staircase")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Two things to carry forward from the picture. First, **each leg is an exact 1-D minimum** —
no step size, no overshoot, the line goes exactly to the tangent point. Second, the
*efficiency* of the staircase is governed by how aligned the contours are with the axes:
axis-aligned (uncoupled) is ideal, tilted (coupled) is slow. That single observation
predicts almost everything about when coordinate descent is the right tool.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## The scheme, precisely {#sec-scheme}

::: {#def-coordinate-descent}
**Definition (cyclic coordinate descent).** Let $f:\mathbb{R}^p\to\mathbb{R}$. Choose a
starting point $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$. At each *sweep* $k=0,1,2,\dots$, update the coordinates
in order $j=1,2,\dots,p$, each time minimizing over that coordinate alone while holding the
others at their **current** values:
$$
x_j^{(k+1)} \;=\; \arg\min_{t\in\mathbb{R}}\;
f\!\bigl(\underbrace{x_1^{(k+1)},\dots,x_{j-1}^{(k+1)}}_{\text{already updated this sweep}},
\; t,\;
\underbrace{x_{j+1}^{(k)},\dots,x_p^{(k)}}_{\text{not yet updated}}\bigr).
$$ {#eq-cd-update}
:::

Two details in @eq-cd-update matter and are worth stating out loud, because they are the
difference between the version that works and a sloppier one.

- **Use the freshest values.** When we update $x_j$, the coordinates $x_1,\dots,x_{j-1}$
  have *already been updated this sweep*, so we use their new values, not the values from
  the start of the sweep. This is the **Gauss–Seidel** style (as opposed to **Jacobi**
  style, which would update every coordinate from the old vector and only commit at the end
  of the sweep). Using fresh values makes each step a genuine descent step on the *current*
  iterate and is what makes the monotone-decrease argument below go through.
- **"$\arg\min_t$" is a one-dimensional problem.** Everything except $t$ is a fixed number,
  so the function being minimized is a function of the single real variable $t$. That is the
  whole point: we have traded one $p$-dimensional problem for a stream of 1-D problems, each
  of which we attack with the Calc-1 machinery of [OPTM-01] (set the 1-D derivative to zero)
  or, at a kink, the subgradient condition of [OPTM-05].

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Does the order in which you sweep the coordinates matter?"* For convergence, the standard
guarantees hold for **cyclic** order (fixed $1,2,\dots,p$), for **random** order (a fresh
random permutation each sweep, or sampling a coordinate at random each step), and for
**greedy** order (the Gauss–Southwell rule: update the coordinate with the largest gradient
component). For *speed*, the choice can matter a lot: randomized coordinate descent has the
cleanest theory and avoids pathological cyclic orderings, while greedy is fastest per step
but costs a full-gradient scan to pick the coordinate. In practice, `glmnet`-style solvers
use cyclic order with **active-set** tricks (skip coordinates that are stuck at zero), which
is fast and simple. The interviewer is probing whether you know that "coordinate descent" is
a *family* indexed by the coordinate-selection rule, not a single algorithm.
:::

## The exact update for a quadratic {#sec-quadratic}

The cleanest case — and the one that recovers Gauss–Seidel — is a quadratic. Let
$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac12\,\mathbf{x}^\top A\,\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^\top \mathbf{x},
\qquad A \text{ symmetric positive-definite.}
$$ {#eq-quad}
Positive-definiteness ($A\succ 0$) makes $f$ strictly convex with a unique minimizer, and in
particular makes every diagonal entry $A_{jj}>0$ (take $\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{e}_j$, the $j$-th
standard basis vector: $\mathbf{e}_j^\top A\,\mathbf{e}_j = A_{jj}>0$). We need the 1-D
slice: fix all coordinates but $x_j$ and read $f$ as a function of $t=x_j$ alone.

Write $A_{jk}$ for the entries of $A$ and $b_j$ for those of $\mathbf{b}$. Expanding the
quadratic form, the only places $x_j$ appears are the diagonal term $\tfrac12 A_{jj}x_j^2$,
the cross terms $A_{jk}x_jx_k$ for $k\ne j$ (each off-diagonal pair $jk$ and $kj$ contributes
$A_{jk}x_jx_k$, and $A$ is symmetric so the two are equal — together $A_{jk}x_jx_k$ summed
over $k\ne j$), and the linear term $-b_j x_j$. Collecting them, with everything else folded
into a constant $C$ that does not involve $x_j$:
$$
f \;=\; \tfrac12 A_{jj}\,x_j^2 \;+\; x_j\!\sum_{k\ne j} A_{jk}\,x_k \;-\; b_j\,x_j \;+\; C.
$$
This is an ordinary upward parabola in $x_j$ (coefficient $\tfrac12 A_{jj}>0$). Differentiate
with respect to $x_j$ and set to zero — the Calc-1 first-order condition of [OPTM-01]:
$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_j}
= A_{jj}\,x_j + \sum_{k\ne j} A_{jk}\,x_k - b_j = 0.
$$
Solving for $x_j$ gives the **exact coordinate update**:
$$
\boxed{\;
x_j \;\leftarrow\; \frac{\,b_j - \sum_{k\ne j} A_{jk}\,x_k\,}{A_{jj}}.
\;}
$$ {#eq-quad-update}
Read it: one inner product against the current $\mathbf{x}$ (excluding the diagonal), one
subtraction, one division. **No step size.** That single division *is* the exact 1-D
minimizer. And @eq-quad-update is precisely the **Gauss–Seidel iteration** for the linear
system $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ — which is the same system as $\nabla f=A\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{b}=\mathbf{0}$,
the stationarity condition of [OPTM-02]. Coordinate descent on a quadratic and Gauss–Seidel
on the normal equations are *the same algorithm*. (The code in @fig-staircase implemented
exactly @eq-quad-update.)

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why is there no step size here, when gradient descent needed one?"* Because we solved the
1-D subproblem **exactly**, not approximately. Gradient descent replaces $f$ by its
first-order Taylor model and trusts it only for a short step $\eta$ — the step size is the
amount of trust you put in a *local linear* approximation. Coordinate descent on a quadratic
uses the *exact* shape of the 1-D slice (it is a parabola, and we know its vertex in closed
form), so there is nothing to trust partially and no step size to choose. When the 1-D slice
is *not* exactly solvable, one does take a single 1-D step instead — and *then* a step size
reappears — but for quadratics and the lasso the exact solve is available.
:::

## Monotone descent, and convergence in the smooth case {#sec-convergence}

Does cycling these exact 1-D minimizations actually reach the joint minimum? For the smooth
case the argument is clean and we give it in full.

::: {#thm-smooth-convergence}
**Theorem (convergence, smooth strongly convex case).** Let $f:\mathbb{R}^p\to\mathbb{R}$ be
continuously differentiable and strongly convex (e.g. the quadratic @eq-quad with
$A\succ 0$). Then cyclic coordinate descent @eq-cd-update produces iterates whose objective
values decrease monotonically, and the iterates converge to the unique global minimizer
$\mathbf{x}^\star$.
:::

::: {.proof}
We argue in three moves: the values can only go down, the limit is stationary, and for a
convex $f$ stationary means the global minimum.

**Move 1 — monotone decrease.** Each coordinate step *minimizes* $f$ over the line through
the current point in the $x_j$ direction. Minimizing can never increase the value: the new
point is the best on that line, and the old point was *on* that line, so
$$
f(\text{after updating }x_j) \;\le\; f(\text{before updating }x_j).
$$
Chaining these inequalities over all $p$ coordinates of a sweep and over all sweeps, the
sequence of objective values $f(\mathbf{x}^{(0)}), f(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}),\dots$ is
**nonincreasing**. It is also bounded below (a strongly convex $f$ has a finite minimum), so
it converges to some limit value $f^\infty$. The iterates themselves stay in a bounded set
(strong convexity: sublevel sets $\{f\le f(\mathbf{x}^{(0)})\}$ are bounded), so by the
Bolzano–Weierstrass theorem some subsequence converges to a limit point $\bar{\mathbf{x}}$.

**Move 2 — the limit is stationary.** At the limit point $\bar{\mathbf{x}}$, consider any
single coordinate $j$. Because each step took $x_j$ to the *exact* minimizer of the 1-D
slice, the 1-D first-order condition holds in the limit:
$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_j}(\bar{\mathbf{x}}) = 0.
$$
Here is the one place smoothness is essential, so we say why carefully. The update sets the
$j$-th partial derivative to zero *using the freshest values of the other coordinates*. As
the iterates converge, those "freshest values" themselves converge to the components of
$\bar{\mathbf{x}}$, and because $f$ is continuously differentiable, the partial derivative
$\partial f/\partial x_j$ is a *continuous* function of all coordinates. A continuous
function that is driven to zero along a convergent sequence is zero at the limit. This holds
for every $j$, so **all** partial derivatives vanish at $\bar{\mathbf{x}}$:
$$
\nabla f(\bar{\mathbf{x}}) = \mathbf{0}.
$$

**Move 3 — stationary $\Rightarrow$ global minimum.** For a convex differentiable function,
a point with zero gradient is a global minimizer — this is the headline theorem of
[OPTM-04], which we may cite as an imported prerequisite. (Sketch of why, for completeness:
convexity gives the tangent-below inequality $f(\mathbf{y})\ge f(\bar{\mathbf{x}}) +
\nabla f(\bar{\mathbf{x}})^\top(\mathbf{y}-\bar{\mathbf{x}})$ for all $\mathbf{y}$; with
$\nabla f(\bar{\mathbf{x}})=\mathbf{0}$ the last term drops and $f(\mathbf{y})\ge
f(\bar{\mathbf{x}})$ everywhere.) Strong convexity makes the minimizer unique, so
$\bar{\mathbf{x}}=\mathbf{x}^\star$; since every convergent subsequence has the same unique
limit, the *whole* sequence converges to $\mathbf{x}^\star$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The hinge of the whole proof is **Move 2**, and it deserves a flag: it used continuity of
$\nabla f$. When $f$ is *not* differentiable — when there are kinks — that step can fail, and
the algorithm can converge to a point where every 1-D slice is minimized yet the *joint*
gradient does not vanish. That is the failure we build next, and the lasso's escape from it
is the article's payoff.

# The honest catch: coordinate descent can jam {#sec-jam}

## A non-smooth function where every axis move is stuck, yet we are not at the minimum {#sec-counterexample}

Here is the cautionary counterexample, in the spirit of Warga (1963) and a staple of the
optimization literature. We do not need a designer function; the *mechanism* shows up in the
simplest possible **non-separable** non-smooth example, where the kink runs *diagonally*
across the two coordinates. Take
$$
\boxed{\,f(x_1,x_2) \;=\; 3\bigl(x_1^2 + x_2^2\bigr) \;+\; 5\,|x_1 - x_2| \;-\; (x_1 + x_2)\,.}
$$ {#eq-jam}
This is convex: a positive-definite quadratic, plus a (convex) absolute value of the *linear*
form $x_1-x_2$, plus a linear term. So it has a unique global minimum. Where is it? Away from
the diagonal the gradient must vanish; on the diagonal $x_1=x_2$ the kink takes over. Set
$x_1=x_2=t$ and the kink term is zero, leaving $6t^2 - 2t$, minimized at $t=\tfrac16$. One
checks (and we will, numerically) that the true minimizer is
$$
\mathbf{x}^\star = \bigl(\tfrac16,\ \tfrac16\bigr), \qquad f(\mathbf{x}^\star) = -\tfrac16.
$$
The crucial structural fact: the non-smooth term $5|x_1-x_2|$ has its kink along the **whole
diagonal** $x_1=x_2$ — a *diagonal ridge that crosses both coordinates*, exactly the
non-separable shape that breaks @thm-smooth-convergence's Move 2. Now run coordinate descent
and watch it get trapped on that ridge **before** reaching the bottom of it.

Start at $(3,-3)$ and update $x_1$ first, holding $x_2=-3$. The 1-D slice is, dropping
constants, $g(t) = 3t^2 - t + 5\,|t - (-3)| = 3t^2 - t + 5|t+3|$. Use the subgradient
condition $0\in g'(t)$. The smooth part has derivative $6t-1$; the kink term contributes
$5\cdot\operatorname{sign}(t+3)$, which is $+5$ for $t>-3$. So for $t>-3$ we need
$6t-1+5=0$, i.e. $t=-\tfrac46=-\tfrac23$ — consistent, since $-\tfrac23>-3$. Thus
$x_1\leftarrow-\tfrac23$. Then update $x_2$ holding $x_1=-\tfrac23$. We can either redo the
calculation or notice that @eq-jam is **unchanged when we swap $x_1\leftrightarrow x_2$**
(both $x_1^2+x_2^2$ and $x_1+x_2$ are symmetric, and $|x_1-x_2|=|x_2-x_1|$), so the
$x_2$-slice with $x_1=-\tfrac23$ is the *identical* 1-D problem we just solved with the roles
of the two variables interchanged: minimize $3t^2 - t + 5|t-(-\tfrac23)|$. For $t>-\tfrac23$
its derivative is $6t-1+5=0$, giving $t=-\tfrac23$ again (and $-\tfrac23$ is the boundary of
that region, which the next paragraph confirms is exactly the optimum). So
$x_2\leftarrow-\tfrac23$, and we have landed on the diagonal at $(-\tfrac23,-\tfrac23)$.

Now the trap springs. At a diagonal point $x_1=x_2=c$, try to update $x_1$: minimize
$h(t)=3t^2 - t + 5|t-c|$. The kink is *at* $t=c$, where the subdifferential of $|t-c|$ is the
whole interval $[-1,1]$. The optimality condition $0\in 6t-1+5[-1,1]$ evaluated at $t=c$ is
$0\in[6c-6,\,6c+4]$, i.e.
$$
-\tfrac23 \;\le\; c \;\le\; 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t=c \text{ is optimal — the coordinate cannot move.}
$$
Our point $c=-\tfrac23$ sits *exactly* at the left end of this interval, so **the $x_1$-update
returns $-\tfrac23$ unchanged**; and because $f$ is unchanged under swapping
$x_1\leftrightarrow x_2$, the $x_2$-update at the same point is the same frozen 1-D problem
and likewise returns $-\tfrac23$. Coordinate descent
is frozen at $(-\tfrac23,-\tfrac23)$, $f=4$ — while the true minimum is way down the same
diagonal at $(\tfrac16,\tfrac16)$, $f=-\tfrac16$. No single-coordinate move helps; only a
*joint* diagonal move (increasing both $x_1$ and $x_2$ together) descends, and coordinate
descent is forbidden from making it. @fig-jam shows the trap.

```{python}
#| label: fig-jam
#| fig-cap: "Coordinate descent jamming on a non-smooth, non-separable objective $f(x_1,x_2)=3(x_1^2+x_2^2)+5|x_1-x_2|-(x_1+x_2)$. The kink of $|x_1-x_2|$ runs along the *diagonal ridge* $x_1=x_2$ (green dotted), and the true minimum (green dot) sits on that ridge at $(\\tfrac16,\\tfrac16)$. Starting from $(3,-3)$, exact coordinate descent (red) snaps onto the ridge and then **freezes** at $(-\\tfrac23,-\\tfrac23)$ (black X): no horizontal or vertical move lowers $f$, because the $[-1,1]$ subdifferential of the kink pins each coordinate. Only a *joint* diagonal move descends the ridge toward the true minimum — and coordinate descent cannot make it. This is the failure that separability prevents."
def f_jam(x1, x2):
    return 3 * (x1 ** 2 + x2 ** 2) + 5 * np.abs(x1 - x2) - (x1 + x2)

def cd_1d_min_jam(other):
    """Exact 1-D minimizer of t -> 3 t^2 + 5|t - other| - t (the slice in either coordinate;
    f is symmetric in x1, x2, so the same slice serves both)."""
    ts = np.linspace(-4, 4, 80001)
    vals = 3 * ts ** 2 + 5 * np.abs(ts - other) - ts
    return ts[np.argmin(vals)]

x = np.array([3.0, -3.0])
path = [x.copy()]
for _ in range(6):
    x[0] = cd_1d_min_jam(x[1]); path.append(x.copy())
    x[1] = cd_1d_min_jam(x[0]); path.append(x.copy())
path = np.array(path)

g = np.linspace(-3.2, 3.2, 320)
X1, X2 = np.meshgrid(g, g)
F = f_jam(X1, X2)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8, 5.2))
ax.contour(X1, X2, F, levels=np.linspace(F.min(), 40, 22), colors=GREY, linewidths=0.6, alpha=0.6)
ax.plot([-3.2, 3.2], [-3.2, 3.2], color=ACCENT, ls=":", lw=1.5, label="ridge $x_1=x_2$")
ax.plot(path[:, 0], path[:, 1], "-o", color=PATH, ms=4, lw=1.6, label="coordinate descent")
ax.scatter(1 / 6, 1 / 6, color=ACCENT, s=120, zorder=6, edgecolor="white",
           label=r"true min $(\frac{1}{6},\frac{1}{6})$")
ax.scatter(*path[0], color=CURVE, s=60, zorder=6, label="start $(3,-3)$")
ax.scatter(*path[-1], color="black", marker="X", s=120, zorder=7, label="stall point")
ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
ax.set_title("Coordinate descent stalls at a non-minimizer")
ax.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.95)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"stall point  ~ ({path[-1,0]:.3f}, {path[-1,1]:.3f}),  f = {f_jam(*path[-1]):.4f}")
print(f"true minimum   (0.1667, 0.1667),  f = {f_jam(1/6, 1/6):.4f}")
```

The printed numbers make it explicit: coordinate descent halts at a point with $f$ strictly
above the minimum, even though *no single-coordinate move decreases $f$ at that point*. It is
stuck on the diagonal ridge $x_1=x_2$, which is where the kink of $|x_1-x_2|$ cuts across the
quadratic bowl. To make progress you would have to move **both** coordinates at once (down the
ridge toward the origin), and coordinate descent is forbidden from doing that.

## Why exactly does it jam? The optimality condition in coordinates {#sec-why-jam}

The diagnosis is precise, and it is the most important conceptual point in the article. For a
smooth $f$, "all partial derivatives are zero" is equivalent to "the full gradient is zero,"
because the gradient is just the *vector* of partials — minimizing every 1-D slice forces
$\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$, the joint optimality condition. For a **non-smooth** convex $f$, the
optimality condition is no longer $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ but the subgradient condition of
[OPTM-05],
$$
\mathbf{0} \in \partial f(\mathbf{x}^\star)
\qquad(\text{the subdifferential, the set of all subgradients}),
$$
and the subdifferential of a general non-smooth function **does not decompose coordinatewise**.
"Optimal in $x_1$ alone" gives $0\in\partial_{x_1}f$, and likewise $0\in\partial_{x_2}f$, but
the *Cartesian product* $\partial_{x_1}f \times \partial_{x_2}f$ can be strictly larger than
the joint subdifferential $\partial f$. So "minimal along every axis" can hold while
"$\mathbf{0}\in\partial f$ jointly" fails — there is a descent direction (the diagonal) that no
axis sees. That gap is the jam.

The escape is now visible in advance. **The gap closes if the non-smooth part is separable.**
If $f(\mathbf{x}) = h(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j} g_j(x_j)$ with $h$ smooth and *convex* and each
$g_j$ a convex function of its **own coordinate only**, then the kinks live one-per-axis —
there is no diagonal ridge crossing coordinates — and the coordinatewise optimality conditions
*do* reassemble into the joint one. This is exactly the structure of the lasso. We state it as
the theorem the whole field rests on.

::: {#thm-separable}
**Theorem (Tseng, 2001 — convergence with a separable non-smooth term).** Let
$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = h(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} g_j(x_j),
$$
where $h$ is convex and continuously differentiable and each $g_j:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ is
convex (possibly non-differentiable, e.g. $g_j(x_j)=\lambda|x_j|$). Then every limit point of
cyclic coordinate descent is a global minimizer of $f$. The jam of @sec-counterexample cannot
occur.
:::

::: {.proof}
We give the heart of the argument — why coordinatewise optimality now implies joint
optimality — and flag the imported piece. Suppose $\bar{\mathbf{x}}$ is a point that minimizes
$f$ along every coordinate axis: for each $j$, $\bar x_j$ minimizes $t\mapsto
h(\dots,t,\dots) + g_j(t)$. By the 1-D subgradient optimality condition of [OPTM-05]
($0\in\partial(\text{1-D objective})$, and the subdifferential of a sum is the sum of
subdifferentials for these well-behaved convex functions), this says
$$
0 \in \partial_{x_j}\!\bigl[h + g_j\bigr](\bar{\mathbf{x}})
= \frac{\partial h}{\partial x_j}(\bar{\mathbf{x}}) + \partial g_j(\bar x_j)
\qquad\text{for every } j.
\tag{$\ast$}
$$
That is, for each $j$ there exists a subgradient $s_j \in \partial g_j(\bar x_j)$ with
$\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x_j}(\bar{\mathbf{x}}) + s_j = 0$.

Now assemble the joint condition. Because the non-smooth part is **separable**, its
subdifferential is exactly the Cartesian product of the one-dimensional subdifferentials:
$$
\partial\!\Bigl[\textstyle\sum_j g_j\Bigr](\bar{\mathbf{x}})
= \partial g_1(\bar x_1)\times\cdots\times \partial g_p(\bar x_p).
$$
This is the key structural fact, and it holds *because* each $g_j$ depends on its own
coordinate alone (the kink of $g_j$ is axis-aligned). Therefore the vector
$\mathbf{s}=(s_1,\dots,s_p)$ built coordinatewise above is a genuine *joint* subgradient of
$\sum_j g_j$ at $\bar{\mathbf{x}}$. And $h$ is smooth, so its only subgradient is its
gradient. The subdifferential of the sum $h + \sum_j g_j$ is the (Minkowski) sum of
subdifferentials — the sum rule for convex functions, imported from [OPTM-05] — so
$$
\nabla h(\bar{\mathbf{x}}) + \mathbf{s}
\;\in\; \partial f(\bar{\mathbf{x}}),
\qquad\text{and by } (\ast)\ \text{ this vector is } \mathbf{0}.
$$
Hence $\mathbf{0}\in\partial f(\bar{\mathbf{x}})$ — the joint optimality condition holds, so
$\bar{\mathbf{x}}$ is a global minimizer of the convex $f$. The coordinatewise optima
reassembled into a joint optimum precisely because separability made the product of
1-D subdifferentials *equal* (not merely contain) the joint one. The full convergence claim
(that the iterates actually reach such a point) follows from the monotone-decrease argument
of @thm-smooth-convergence applied to this setting; the non-separable counterexample
@eq-jam fails this proof exactly at the boxed equality, where a cross-coordinate kink makes
the product strictly *larger* than the joint subdifferential. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Coordinate descent is not a universal minimizer for non-smooth problems.** The mistake is
to assume that "minimize each coordinate to optimality and cycle" always finds the joint
minimum — it does *not* when the non-smoothness couples coordinates. The counterexample
@eq-jam is the permanent warning. Before reaching for coordinate descent on a non-smooth
objective, check that the non-smooth part is **separable**: a sum of functions each touching
one coordinate (or one disjoint block of coordinates — *block* coordinate descent handles
group penalties the same way). The lasso passes this test; a fused lasso or total-variation
penalty (which couples *neighboring* coordinates through $|x_{j}-x_{j+1}|$) does **not**, and
plain coordinate descent can stall on it — those need the proximal/dual methods of [OPTM-16].
:::

# The payoff: coordinate descent solves the lasso {#sec-lasso}

## The lasso objective and why it is the sweet spot {#sec-lasso-objective}

The **lasso** (Regression's [REGR-15]) fits a linear model by minimizing squared error plus
an $\ell_1$ penalty that drives coefficients to *exactly* zero, producing sparse, interpretable
models. With design matrix $X\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times p}$ (columns $\mathbf{x}_{\cdot 1},\dots,
\mathbf{x}_{\cdot p}$), response $\mathbf{y}\in\mathbb{R}^n$, and penalty $\lambda>0$:
$$
f(\boldsymbol\beta)
= \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2n}\,\|\mathbf{y} - X\boldsymbol\beta\|_2^2}_{\text{smooth } h(\boldsymbol\beta)}
\;+\; \underbrace{\lambda\sum_{j=1}^{p}|\beta_j|}_{\text{separable non-smooth }\sum_j g_j(\beta_j)} .
$$ {#eq-lasso}
Look at the two pieces against @thm-separable. The squared-error term $h$ is smooth and
convex (it is a quadratic in $\boldsymbol\beta$). The penalty $\lambda\sum_j|\beta_j|$ is a
**sum of one-coordinate functions** $g_j(\beta_j)=\lambda|\beta_j|$ — *exactly* the separable
structure the theorem demands. So coordinate descent on the lasso **cannot jam**: it provably
converges to the global minimum, and — the gift — each 1-D subproblem has a closed-form
solution that *automatically produces zeros*. We derive that solution now.

## The scalar lasso subproblem and soft-thresholding {#sec-soft}

Freeze all coefficients but $\beta_j$ and minimize @eq-lasso over $\beta_j$ alone. Let us
reduce the 1-D slice to its essential shape. Write the **partial residual** — the part of
$\mathbf{y}$ not explained by the *other* predictors —
$$
\mathbf{r}^{(-j)} = \mathbf{y} - \sum_{k\ne j}\beta_k\,\mathbf{x}_{\cdot k},
$$
so that $\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta = \mathbf{r}^{(-j)} - \beta_j\,\mathbf{x}_{\cdot j}$.
Then, dropping additive constants that do not involve $\beta_j$, the 1-D objective is
$$
\phi(\beta_j) = \frac{1}{2n}\bigl\|\mathbf{r}^{(-j)} - \beta_j\,\mathbf{x}_{\cdot j}\bigr\|_2^2
+ \lambda|\beta_j|.
$$
Expand the squared norm, $\|\mathbf{r}^{(-j)} - \beta_j\mathbf{x}_{\cdot j}\|^2 =
\|\mathbf{r}^{(-j)}\|^2 - 2\beta_j\,\mathbf{x}_{\cdot j}^\top\mathbf{r}^{(-j)} +
\beta_j^2\,\|\mathbf{x}_{\cdot j}\|^2$. Assume the columns are standardized so that
$\tfrac1n\|\mathbf{x}_{\cdot j}\|^2 = 1$ (the universal convention in lasso solvers; it makes
the penalty comparable across predictors). Define the single number
$$
\rho_j = \tfrac{1}{n}\,\mathbf{x}_{\cdot j}^\top\,\mathbf{r}^{(-j)}
$$
— the (scaled) correlation of predictor $j$ with the current partial residual, i.e. the
ordinary least-squares coefficient predictor $j$ *would* take if it alone fit the leftover.
Dropping constants, the slice becomes the clean scalar problem
$$
\phi(\beta_j) \;=\; \tfrac12\,\beta_j^2 \;-\; \rho_j\,\beta_j \;+\; \lambda|\beta_j|
\;=\; \tfrac12\,(\beta_j - \rho_j)^2 \;+\; \lambda|\beta_j| \;+\; \text{const}.
$$ {#eq-scalar-lasso}
This is the canonical scalar problem $\min_\beta\ \tfrac12(\beta-\rho)^2 + \lambda|\beta|$ —
"stay near $\rho$, but pay $\lambda$ per unit of $|\beta|$." Now we minimize it exactly. The
absolute value is the one kink; we handle it with the subgradient optimality condition of
[OPTM-05], $0\in\partial\phi$. The subdifferential of $|\beta|$ is
$$
\partial|\beta| = \begin{cases}\{+1\} & \beta>0,\\ \{-1\} & \beta<0,\\ [-1,1] & \beta=0,\end{cases}
$$
the smooth derivative away from zero, and the whole interval $[-1,1]$ *at* the kink (every
slope in $[-1,1]$ supports the V from below at the origin). The optimality condition
$0\in\partial\phi(\beta) = (\beta-\rho) + \lambda\,\partial|\beta|$ splits into three cases.

**Case $\beta>0$:** then $\partial|\beta|=\{1\}$ and we need $(\beta-\rho)+\lambda=0$, i.e.
$\beta=\rho-\lambda$. This is consistent ($\beta>0$) **iff** $\rho>\lambda$.

**Case $\beta<0$:** then $\partial|\beta|=\{-1\}$ and we need $(\beta-\rho)-\lambda=0$, i.e.
$\beta=\rho+\lambda$. Consistent ($\beta<0$) **iff** $\rho<-\lambda$.

**Case $\beta=0$:** then $\partial|\beta|=[-1,1]$ and we need $0\in(0-\rho)+\lambda[-1,1]
=[-\rho-\lambda,\,-\rho+\lambda]$, i.e. $-\rho-\lambda\le 0\le -\rho+\lambda$, which is
$|\rho|\le\lambda$. So whenever the correlation $\rho$ is **smaller in magnitude than the
penalty $\lambda$**, the minimizer is *exactly zero* — the kink at the origin "captures" the
solution. This is the mechanism by which the lasso sets coefficients to *exactly* zero, seen
here in its purest form.

Collecting the three cases gives the closed-form minimizer, the **soft-thresholding
operator**:
$$
\boxed{\;
\beta_j \;\leftarrow\; S_\lambda(\rho_j)
\;=\;
\operatorname{sign}(\rho_j)\,\bigl(|\rho_j| - \lambda\bigr)_+
\;=\;
\begin{cases}
\rho_j - \lambda, & \rho_j > \lambda,\\[2pt]
0, & |\rho_j| \le \lambda,\\[2pt]
\rho_j + \lambda, & \rho_j < -\lambda,
\end{cases}
\;}
$$ {#eq-soft}
where $(z)_+=\max(0,z)$. In words: **shrink $\rho_j$ toward zero by $\lambda$, and clip
anything within $\lambda$ of zero to exactly zero.** @fig-soft draws the operator next to the
"hard" alternative.

```{python}
#| label: fig-soft
#| fig-cap: "The soft-thresholding operator $S_\\lambda(\\rho)=\\operatorname{sign}(\\rho)(|\\rho|-\\lambda)_+$ (red), the exact solution of the scalar lasso problem $\\min_\\beta \\tfrac12(\\beta-\\rho)^2+\\lambda|\\beta|$. It is the identity (grey dashed) shifted toward zero by $\\lambda$, with a flat **dead zone** $|\\rho|\\le\\lambda$ where the answer is exactly $0$. That dead zone is *why the lasso produces sparsity*: any predictor whose correlation with the partial residual is below the penalty is zeroed out entirely. The 'hard' threshold (green) — used by best-subset / $\\ell_0$ ideas — keeps values unshrunk but discontinuous; soft-thresholding is the convex, continuous relaxation coordinate descent delivers for free."
def soft(rho, lam):
    return np.sign(rho) * np.maximum(np.abs(rho) - lam, 0.0)

def hard(rho, lam):
    out = rho.copy()
    out[np.abs(rho) <= lam] = 0.0
    return out

rho = np.linspace(-3, 3, 600)
lam = 1.0
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6, 4.6))
ax.plot(rho, rho, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.4, label=r"identity $\beta=\rho$")
ax.plot(rho, soft(rho, lam), color=PATH, lw=2.4, label=r"soft-threshold $S_\lambda(\rho)$")
ax.plot(rho, hard(rho, lam), color=ACCENT, lw=1.6, alpha=0.8, label="hard threshold")
ax.axvspan(-lam, lam, color=GREY, alpha=0.12)
ax.text(0, 2.4, "dead zone\n$|\\rho|\\leq\\lambda$", ha="center", fontsize=9, color=GREY)
ax.axhline(0, color="k", lw=0.6); ax.axvline(0, color="k", lw=0.6)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\rho$ (correlation with partial residual)")
ax.set_ylabel(r"updated coefficient $\beta$")
ax.set_title(r"Soft-thresholding ($\lambda=1$)")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.95)
plt.tight_layout()
```

So the lasso coordinate-descent algorithm is, in full: **for each coordinate $j$ in turn,
compute the correlation $\rho_j$ of predictor $j$ with the current partial residual, then set
$\beta_j\leftarrow S_\lambda(\rho_j)$, and cycle until the coefficients stop changing.** No
step size, no matrix inversion, automatic sparsity, and (by @thm-separable) a guaranteed
global optimum. This is the `glmnet` engine.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why does the lasso give exact zeros when ridge regression ($\ell_2$ penalty) does not?"*
Run the same scalar derivation with $g_j(\beta_j)=\lambda\beta_j^2$ instead of
$\lambda|\beta_j|$. There is **no kink** — the penalty is smooth — so the optimality
condition is the ordinary $(\beta-\rho)+2\lambda\beta=0$, giving $\beta=\rho/(1+2\lambda)$.
That is a *proportional* shrinkage: every coefficient is multiplied by $1/(1+2\lambda)<1$,
which pulls them toward zero but **never makes them exactly zero** (it would take
$\lambda=\infty$). The lasso's exact zeros come entirely from the *non-differentiable* corner
of $|\beta|$ at the origin: the interval subdifferential $[-1,1]$ creates the dead zone
$|\rho|\le\lambda$ in @fig-soft. Sparsity is a gift of the kink, and coordinate descent is the
method that cashes it in cheaply. (This is also why the lasso *needs* the subgradient
machinery of [OPTM-05] while ridge does not.)
:::

## Worked example: a sparse high-dimensional regression {#sec-worked-lasso}

Now the honest end-to-end run, on the regime where the lasso belongs: $p$ large, $n$ modest,
and a truly sparse signal (only a few predictors matter). We implement the coordinate-descent
lasso from @eq-soft *ourselves* — the math is primary, so we want to see every update — and
recover the sparse coefficient vector.

```{python}
#| label: fig-lasso-fit
#| fig-cap: "Coordinate-descent lasso on a sparse, high-dimensional problem ($n=120$, $p=200$, only $6$ nonzero true coefficients). **Left:** the objective $f(\\boldsymbol\\beta)=\\tfrac{1}{2n}\\|\\mathbf{y}-X\\boldsymbol\\beta\\|^2+\\lambda\\|\\boldsymbol\\beta\\|_1$ versus full coordinate sweeps — it drops monotonically (@thm-smooth-convergence's descent property) and converges in a handful of sweeps. **Right:** the recovered coefficients (red) against the truth (green circles). The lasso has zeroed out the $194$ irrelevant predictors exactly (the soft-threshold dead zone) and recovered the $6$ true ones, shrunk slightly toward zero — the honest price of the $\\ell_1$ penalty."
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n, p, k = 120, 200, 6
X = rng.standard_normal((n, p))
X = (X - X.mean(0)) / X.std(0)                  # standardize columns: (1/n)||x_j||^2 = 1
beta_true = np.zeros(p)
support = rng.choice(p, size=k, replace=False)
beta_true[support] = rng.uniform(1.5, 3.0, size=k) * rng.choice([-1, 1], size=k)
y = X @ beta_true + rng.standard_normal(n) * 0.5
y = y - y.mean()

def soft_thresh(z, lam):
    return np.sign(z) * max(abs(z) - lam, 0.0)

def lasso_cd(X, y, lam, n_sweeps=40):
    n, p = X.shape
    beta = np.zeros(p)
    r = y - X @ beta                            # full residual, kept up to date
    obj = []
    for _ in range(n_sweeps):
        for j in range(p):
            # add predictor j back into the residual -> partial residual
            r = r + X[:, j] * beta[j]
            rho = (X[:, j] @ r) / n             # correlation with partial residual
            beta[j] = soft_thresh(rho, lam)
            r = r - X[:, j] * beta[j]           # remove the updated contribution
        obj.append(0.5 / n * (r @ r) + lam * np.abs(beta).sum())
    return beta, np.array(obj)

lam = 0.15
beta_hat, obj = lasso_cd(X, y, lam, n_sweeps=30)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 3.9))
axL.plot(np.arange(1, len(obj) + 1), obj, "-o", color=PATH, ms=3.5)
axL.set_xlabel("coordinate sweep"); axL.set_ylabel(r"objective $f(\beta)$")
axL.set_title("Objective decreases monotonically"); axL.set_yscale("log")

idx = np.arange(p)
axR.scatter(idx, beta_hat, color=PATH, s=14, label=r"lasso $\hat\beta$")
axR.scatter(support, beta_true[support], facecolors="none", edgecolors=ACCENT,
            s=80, lw=1.8, label="true nonzeros")
axR.axhline(0, color="k", lw=0.6)
axR.set_xlabel("predictor index $j$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"coefficient")
axR.set_title("Recovered sparse coefficients"); axR.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.95)
plt.tight_layout()
nnz = int(np.sum(np.abs(beta_hat) > 1e-8))
print(f"nonzero coefficients found: {nnz}  (true support size: {k})")
print(f"selected indices: {sorted(idx[np.abs(beta_hat) > 1e-8].tolist())}")
print(f"true     indices: {sorted(support.tolist())}")
```

@fig-lasso-fit and its printout show the algorithm selected a small set of predictors very close to the true
support, with the objective falling monotonically and flattening within a few sweeps. Note the
implementation detail that makes coordinate descent *fast* on this problem: we keep the full
residual $\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta$ updated incrementally — adding predictor
$j$ back in, computing $\rho_j$ as one inner product, then removing the new contribution — so
each coordinate update is $O(n)$ work rather than recomputing $X\boldsymbol\beta$ from
scratch. Across $p$ coordinates a sweep costs $O(np)$, and because most coefficients are zero,
*active-set* tracking (skipping the zeros) makes later sweeps far cheaper still. That cheap,
sparsity-aware bookkeeping is precisely why coordinate descent beats gradient descent on the
lasso.

## The regularization path, for free {#sec-path}

The final reason coordinate descent dominates lasso solving: it computes the **whole
regularization path** — the solution $\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}(\lambda)$ as $\lambda$ ranges from
large (everything zero) down to small (a dense fit) — almost as cheaply as a single solve,
using **warm starts**. Solve at a large $\lambda$ (trivial: all coefficients zero), then
decrease $\lambda$ slightly and *start coordinate descent from the previous solution*. Because
the solution moves only a little as $\lambda$ changes, each warm-started solve takes very few
sweeps. @fig-path traces it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-path
#| fig-cap: "The lasso regularization path, computed by warm-started coordinate descent ($n=120$, $p=200$, $6$ true nonzeros). Each curve is one coefficient $\\hat\\beta_j(\\lambda)$ as the penalty $\\lambda$ decreases (left to right, log scale). At large $\\lambda$ (left) all coefficients are zero; as $\\lambda$ shrinks, predictors enter one by one. The six true predictors (colored, solid) enter early and grow; the $194$ noise predictors (grey) stay near zero until $\\lambda$ is very small. Coordinate descent produces this entire family of models in barely more time than a single fit, because each solve warm-starts from its neighbor — the property that made `glmnet` the standard lasso tool."
lams = np.logspace(np.log10(1.0), np.log10(0.02), 40)
beta = np.zeros(p)
paths = np.zeros((len(lams), p))
for i, lam in enumerate(lams):
    beta, _ = lasso_cd(X, y, lam, n_sweeps=12)   # warm start: reuse previous beta
    paths[i] = beta

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.4))
for j in range(p):
    if j in support:
        ax.plot(lams, paths[:, j], color=PATH, lw=1.8, zorder=3)
    else:
        ax.plot(lams, paths[:, j], color=GREY, lw=0.6, alpha=0.5, zorder=1)
ax.set_xscale("log"); ax.invert_xaxis()
ax.set_xlabel(r"penalty $\lambda$ (log scale, decreasing $\rightarrow$)")
ax.set_ylabel(r"coefficient $\hat\beta_j(\lambda)$")
ax.set_title("Lasso regularization path via warm-started coordinate descent")
ax.axhline(0, color="k", lw=0.6)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The path is the practitioner's real deliverable: you rarely know the right $\lambda$ in
advance, so you compute the whole path and pick $\lambda$ by cross-validation. Coordinate
descent's warm-start efficiency is what makes "fit at 40 penalty values and cross-validate"
routine rather than expensive.

# The honest comparison, and the edge of applicability {#sec-comparison}

## Coordinate descent versus gradient descent, head to head {#sec-vs-gd}

Both are first-order methods; the choice between them is a real judgment call, and @fig-compare
makes it concrete on the same lasso problem. We run our coordinate-descent lasso against a
**subgradient method** (the natural gradient-style approach to the non-smooth $\ell_1$ term:
step against a subgradient of $f$) and compare progress per unit of work.

```{python}
#| label: fig-compare
#| fig-cap: "Coordinate descent (red) vs. a subgradient method (grey) on the same lasso objective ($n=120$, $p=200$). The $x$-axis is full passes over the data (one coordinate sweep $\\approx$ one subgradient step in data touched). Coordinate descent drives the objective down fast and converges; the subgradient method, lacking an exact 1-D solve and forced to a decaying step size by the kink, crawls and never produces exact zeros. On a *separable* non-smooth problem with a closed-form coordinate update, coordinate descent is the right tool — this is the honest picture, not a rigged one: subgradient descent is a legitimate but ill-suited choice here."
def subgradient_lasso(X, y, lam, n_steps=30, eta0=0.02):
    n, p = X.shape
    beta = np.zeros(p)
    obj = []
    for t in range(n_steps):
        r = X @ beta - y
        grad_smooth = (X.T @ r) / n
        subgrad = grad_smooth + lam * np.sign(beta)   # sign(0)=0: a valid subgradient choice
        eta = eta0 / np.sqrt(t + 1)                   # decaying step, required at a kink
        beta = beta - eta * subgrad
        obj.append(0.5 / n * (r @ r) + lam * np.abs(beta).sum())
    return beta, np.array(obj)

lam = 0.15
_, obj_cd = lasso_cd(X, y, lam, n_sweeps=30)
beta_sg, obj_sg = subgradient_lasso(X, y, lam, n_steps=30, eta0=0.05)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.2))
ax.plot(np.arange(1, len(obj_cd) + 1), obj_cd, "-o", color=PATH, ms=3.5, label="coordinate descent")
ax.plot(np.arange(1, len(obj_sg) + 1), obj_sg, "-s", color=GD, ms=3.5, label="subgradient method")
ax.set_xlabel("full passes over the data")
ax.set_ylabel(r"objective $f(\beta)$"); ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_title("Coordinate descent dominates on the lasso")
ax.legend(framealpha=0.95)
plt.tight_layout()
nnz_sg = int(np.sum(np.abs(beta_sg) > 1e-8))
print(f"coordinate descent: exact zeros produced (sparse).")
print(f"subgradient method nonzeros (|beta|>1e-8): {nnz_sg} of {p}  -- no exact sparsity")
```

The verdict, stated plainly so you can recite it in an interview:

- **Reach for coordinate descent when** (1) each 1-D subproblem is *cheap and closed-form* —
  a quadratic (one division), or a separable penalty like the lasso/elastic-net (one
  soft-threshold); (2) the non-smooth part is **separable**; (3) the problem is
  **high-dimensional and sparse**, so active-set tracking and warm starts pay off. The lasso,
  elastic net, sparse logistic regression, and graphical-lasso all fit this and use coordinate
  descent in production.
- **Reach for gradient descent / Newton instead when** (1) there is **no cheap closed-form
  coordinate solve** (a generic dense smooth objective, a neural network); (2) the variables
  are **strongly coupled** — tilted, narrow contours — where the axis-aligned staircase of
  @fig-staircase crawls just as gradient descent's zig-zag does, and a direction-aware method
  (momentum [OPTM-08], Newton [OPTM-09]) wins; (3) the non-smooth term is **non-separable**
  (fused lasso, total variation), where coordinate descent can *jam* (@sec-jam) and you need
  proximal/dual methods ([OPTM-16]).

## The edge: when coordinate descent is the wrong tool {#sec-edge}

We have already met the two boundaries; @fig-edge shows the *coupling* one quantitatively,
because it is the failure most likely to bite you silently. We run coordinate descent on a
smooth quadratic as we crank up the off-diagonal coupling (the condition number), and count
the sweeps to convergence.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: "The edge of applicability: coupling kills coordinate descent's speed. For the quadratic $\\tfrac12\\mathbf{x}^\\top A\\mathbf{x}$ with $A$ having unit diagonal and off-diagonal $c$ (so $A=[[1,c],[c,1]]$), the condition number is $(1+c)/(1-c)$, which blows up as the coupling $c\\to 1$. The plot shows sweeps needed to reach $\\|\\mathbf{x}\\|<10^{-4}$ versus condition number (log-log). The count grows steeply with the condition number — the same ill-conditioning dependence that plagues gradient descent ([OPTM-07]). When your variables are highly correlated, neither axis-aligned nor steepest-descent steps help; you need curvature (Newton, [OPTM-09]) or a change of coordinates."
def sweeps_to_converge(c, tol=1e-4, max_sweeps=200000):
    A = np.array([[1.0, c], [c, 1.0]])
    b = np.zeros(2)
    x = np.array([1.0, 1.0])
    for s in range(1, max_sweeps + 1):
        for j in range(2):
            x[j] = (b[j] - (A[j] @ x - A[j, j] * x[j])) / A[j, j]
        if np.linalg.norm(x) < tol:
            return s
    return max_sweeps

cs = np.array([0.0, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 0.95, 0.98, 0.99])
kappa = (1 + cs) / (1 - cs)
sweeps = np.array([sweeps_to_converge(c) for c in cs])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.2, 4.2))
ax.plot(kappa, sweeps, "-o", color=PATH, ms=5)
ax.set_xscale("log"); ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel(r"condition number $\kappa = (1+c)/(1-c)$")
ax.set_ylabel("sweeps to converge")
ax.set_title("Coupling slows coordinate descent (just like GD)")
plt.tight_layout()
```

The lesson lands the same way it did for gradient descent in [OPTM-07]: **first-order,
coordinate-blind methods all suffer when the problem is ill-conditioned.** Coordinate descent
is not a magic escape from the condition number — its escape is from the *step size* and from
*separable non-smoothness*, not from coupling. When coupling is the enemy, you change tools
(Newton, or a preconditioner), not the coordinate-selection rule.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is coordinate descent the same as stochastic gradient descent? They both touch part of the
problem at a time."* No — and the distinction is a favorite probe. **SGD** ([OPTM-11])
subsamples the *data* (the rows / the sum $\tfrac1n\sum_i f_i$): each step uses a few
observations to form a *noisy, unbiased estimate of the full gradient over all parameters*.
**Coordinate descent** subsamples the *parameters* (the columns / the coordinates of
$\mathbf{x}$): each step uses *all* the data but updates only one parameter, *exactly*. SGD's
randomness is in which data points it sees; coordinate descent's per-step choice is which
parameter to move, and the move is typically exact, not noisy. They are orthogonal ideas and
can even be combined. One axis of subsampling is rows, the other is columns.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from the **descent spine** ([OPTM-06] gradient descent, the rival you now know
when to prefer and when not to) and the **convexity spine** ([OPTM-04] convexity, [OPTM-05]
subgradients and the $0\in\partial f$ optimality condition, which gave us soft-thresholding).
Coordinate descent fuses them into a concrete algorithm. From here:

- **[REGR-15 *The Lasso*]** and **[REGR-16 *Elastic Net*]** — the downstream consumers. They
  own the *statistics* of sparse regression (why $\ell_1$ selects variables, the
  bias–variance story, cross-validating $\lambda$) and link *here* for the solver: the
  soft-thresholding update @eq-soft and the warm-started path @fig-path are exactly what
  `glmnet` runs.
- **[OPTM-16 *Proximal and Projected Methods*]** — the generalization. The soft-threshold is
  the **proximal operator of the $\ell_1$ norm**; proximal gradient (ISTA/FISTA) takes one
  prox step over *all* coordinates at once, handling non-separable penalties (fused lasso,
  total variation) that defeat coordinate descent (@sec-jam). Coordinate descent and proximal
  gradient are the two faces of "descent meets a kink."
- **Unsupervised Learning (k-means, matrix factorization)** links here for the broader idea
  of **alternating minimization** — block coordinate descent where each "coordinate" is a
  whole group of variables (the centroids, then the assignments; or one factor matrix, then
  the other). The monotone-descent argument of @thm-smooth-convergence is the backbone of
  those convergence proofs too.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The solver that made it famous.** J. Friedman, T. Hastie, R. Tibshirani, "Regularization
  Paths for Generalized Linear Models via Coordinate Descent," *J. Statistical Software* 33
  (2010) — the `glmnet` paper. It is unusually readable and shows the soft-thresholding update,
  warm starts, and active sets exactly as derived here.
- **The convergence theory.** P. Tseng, "Convergence of a Block Coordinate Descent Method for
  Nondifferentiable Minimization," *J. Optimization Theory and Applications* 109 (2001) — the
  source of @thm-separable and the precise statement of when separability rescues the method.
  S. J. Wright, "Coordinate Descent Algorithms," *Mathematical Programming* 151 (2015) is the
  modern survey, including randomized and greedy variants and their rates.
- **The cautionary classic.** The non-smooth jamming counterexample (@sec-counterexample)
  traces to J. Warga (1963) and is discussed in every serious treatment; Bertsekas, *Nonlinear
  Programming*, gives the general block-descent conditions.
- **Open threads this article deferred.** *Convergence rates* — we proved *that* coordinate
  descent converges but not *how fast*; randomized coordinate descent enjoys clean linear /
  $O(1/k)$ rates (Nesterov, 2012), governed (again) by a condition number, paralleling
  [OPTM-07]. *Block and group versions* — when coordinates come in natural groups (one-hot
  encodings, grouped predictors), **block coordinate descent** with a group-soft-threshold is
  the group-lasso solver; the separability argument of @thm-separable extends block-by-block.
  *The proximal view* — that the soft-threshold *is* a proximal operator is the bridge to
  [OPTM-16], which subsumes coordinate descent into a single unifying framework.
